title: What an American thinks about Estonia

transcript.text: Hello. This video is what an American thinks about Estonia. I was in Estonia last week for like one day. Um, I arrived there at night at like 11 o'clock. It was still kind of light out. It was like July, end of July.

Um, and then I went to the hostel called the, called the Euphoria Hostel. And it's a really cool hostel. It's right near the historical center. And they have like this really cool hangout area, big living room with tapestries everywhere and all kinds of instruments and a huge screen, canvas screen with a little projector and they watch movies.

And the night that I was leaving, the guy, the English guy who was working there was already going, we're, tonight's, tonight's Kung Fu night. Tonight's Kung Fu night. We're cooking up Chinese food. Really cheap, really cheap. And then we're going to watch Kung Fu movies all night long. So, uh, it was cool. Hostel. Um, it was cheap too. It was like 10 euro or something, 12 euro. Uh, yeah, Estonia is cheap. Um, being in Estonia, um, it's hard to believe it's that they were part of the USSR just 20 years ago.

Because it seemed like a really well-developed country. Everybody was totally fluent in English. Um, people were, you know, Scandinavian looking and happy. And, um, it didn't look run down or, you know, I didn't even see any of those big, you know, communist apartment buildings all over the place.
Oh, I'm sure they were there. Maybe I just did in the distance, but I went there right after seeing Vilnius, Lithuania and, uh, Riga, Latvia.
And as I went north, it seemed like the countries got more, or the capital cities got bigger.
Uh, Vilnius was like a little town. Riga was, uh, had a lot of, like, bigger buildings and more people.
Like, Vilnius was, like, empty. And Riga was, uh, totally touristy. Um, and then, uh, Tallinn, Estonia was even bigger.
Or more developed, um, a bigger downtown area and wider streets. Um, more people, more trams and stuff and buses going through.
Um, but it's not a big city. I went over to the top of, um, yeah, the downtown central area of Tallinn was really interesting, too.
Um, just really similar to Riga. Um, only pedestrians walking everywhere.
If you've been to Prague, it's like Prague, but not, not as congested with cars as Prague.
Um, um, and not as many, not as developed, like, as far as McDonald's and, um, subways all over the place.
Um, but it was similar to that. Pedestrian, um, cobblestone streets all over the place and really narrow streets and, um, tourists everywhere.
I heard a lot of Spanish tourists, too, people speaking in Spanish.
Um, and it's really pretty. It's beautiful.
And they have the central downtown area, the historic downtown area, um, that was kind of surrounded by walls.
And you can see the walls going around it with these big towers every 100 yards or something.
Um, circular towers.
And, uh, and then in the middle of the central downtown area, there's this hill with a big wall surrounding that hill.
And it's a smaller area with, uh, another square on the middle of that.
So they have a big square in the, in the down, the historical downtown.
And then another square in the top of the hill inside of the historical downtown, like a historical downtown within a wall inside of the bigger one.
With a tower on the top.
But I went up the tower, um, paid five euros.
And then the guy even actually walked up there and gave me a little tour that I filmed.
Um, and, um, I guess the, um, politicians live up there and their apartments are like 5 million euros or 15 million or something.
But, um, there was a cool museum in one of the towers that I went up and it was really interesting.
I saw the outhouse way up, up, up, up, a few stories up and their sink and cooking area and everything.
Um, but yeah, it's hard to believe they were part of the USSR just 20 years ago.
They, everything was written in Latin.
Um, I did see, you know, some of the scripts like in the touristy areas when you have multiple languages.
One of them wasn't in Russian, but it didn't seem like any people spoke Russian.
Um, I think they were just taken over by the Russians briefly since the World War II.
The USSR went nuts and took over, um, all those other countries.
And I guess nobody stopped them from taking the Baltics.
But that, I guess, uh, historically, ethnically, um, Estonia is part of Scandinavia.
Their language is related to Finnish.
They were, back in the 1700s, they were part of the Swedish Empire, which encompassed, uh, Finland and Sweden.
So then Sweden and Russia had a war, which Russia won and took over Estonia for a little bit.
Then Estonia was its own country.
And then they had another war with, I guess, Russia took them over maybe in 1918 or something like that.
I don't really remember.
And, uh, and then in the war, I guess they had to fight on both sides.
Or maybe that was in Latvia.
But anyway, Latvia and Lithuania, they're a different language.
And then Estonia, which is, I guess, related to Finnish, though.
Or Swedish.
But anyways, yeah, it was wild to think that it's part of the USSR.
Because it's not, it wasn't like Ukraine or Moldova, you know.
Um, but it was still cheap.
A lot cheaper than Helsinki.
Um, but when the people speak to you, they seem, they sound, they have the same kind of accent as, as the Finns.
There's this girl working in my hostel who is from Tallinn, and she was talking like this.
It was, it was cute.
But, um, let's see here.
Um, that's all, I was only there, like, one day.
But it's kind of like a cheaper, I guess a cheaper Finland.
Um, but apart from the central downtown area, historical area, it didn't seem like it was a very big city as far as square miles.
Because I could see the outer edges of it from the tower, and they weren't very far away.
It was only just a couple of miles.
So, as far as size, you know, maybe only, like, five or six, seven miles in diameter, maybe.
Or, that's just the impression I got.
Um, at one point, it did look like there was, like, a moat around parts of it.
Um, on the southeast corner, southwest corner, I saw, like, a moat, remnants of a moat.
And, um, I overheard a tourist guide at one point saying that, like, the river or the ocean used to be right on the edge of this deck, historical downtown.
So, when they originally settled it a long time ago, it was, um, it was right on the edge of, let's see, on a hill.
The downtown area, the modern, modern downtown area with all the skyscrapers I also went to.
And, uh, there were a couple of buildings.
It was kind of like, uh, it was really compact, like it was in, uh, Vilnius.
But there were people walking around.
It was still kind of not as busy, though.
Um, but, we had a lot of cool statues.
A few cool statues.
I saw a statue that was painted.
The eyes were painted in.
I thought that was really cool.
Because, uh, earlier in the day, which I didn't actually manage to upload to YouTube, I lost, like, the first third of my filming of Riga.
Because my card, which filled up, because I had the wrong card in the beginning, fell out of my pocket along with a brand new battery I had for my video camera.
Which was a bummer.
But it was my fault.
I should have put it in my money bag.
And I was stupid.
Put it in my pocket with the map.
But anyways, I said in the video, I go, oh, look at these cool statues.
I wish they painted them.
And then later on in the day, I see my first painted statue.
The eyes were painted.
So that was cool.
So I make a prediction that in the future, all the statues are going to be completely painted, like, realistically.
And someone said that they, that in Roman times, they were all painted.
And that would be really cool.
That would have been really pretty.
I mean, I should do that.
But anyways.
Estonia, first place I saw with painted statues.
And, but it wasn't particularly crowded as far as traffic.
And, and the people seemed to pretty good at English as well.
Generally, I think it seemed like they were all fluent in English.
Which has also made it even harder to believe that it's part of the USSR.
So I, although I think I mainly spoke to, like, younger people.
But, um, yeah, Estonia was cool.
It's a cool place to go to because it's cheap.
And it's, uh, and talent, and talent is really great.
Uh, the, the, um, the bus drive over, it was, it was, like, Lithuania and, uh, and, uh, Latvia.
Although, yeah, actually Latvia and at least Estonia, more than what I saw of Lithuania's,
There were lots of, um, like, pine forests with really thick pine trees, really tall pine trees everywhere.
And it was really flat.
And it was really pretty.
And the roads were in really good shape, too.
And they all had, like, bike, really nice bike paths on the side of them for a lot of it.
Just like in Finland.
So, uh, yeah, it was a nice developed country.
Um, okay.
So that's what an American thinks about Estonia.
